speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sklaver, you just finished telling me the story of the priest who didn't convert you. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the priest story was-- belongs to Miedzyrzecz, the town when we used to sleep over at night in the church. I am now back in Rowne, and Rowne is-- is freed from the Germans. They retreated, and the Soviet government took over. Where, after I met this lady that was from my our town-- had a sister in our town, which was my-- my sister's friend. She was a kindergarten teacher. And their whole family perished, but she survived, and she was very, very pretty. Beautiful, long, long braids. She told me that some of the families of Hoszcza, that I also-- she thinks I have a letter from my sister from-- from Russia. So that could not keep me, definitely, with the Polish family. I thanked them and they packed up and they went to Miedzyrzecz and I worked to Hoszcza. And I did find in Hoszcza the teacher, the one that was our teacher during the Soviet regime. And she was my sister's friend. They went together in gymnasium, and she did have a letter. She married from the town of Ostrog to our town, and she did have a letter from my sister with an address. My sister was in [POLISH], and she was coming back from the Ural Mountains. She found out that her husband survived-- my-- my brother-in-law survived. And they met in [POLISH]. That's the town where she evacuated from with my-- with-- with my uncle and aunt. And then in [POLISH], I visited our maid, our trusted maid Jurina, and she wanted me to go to-- to bring food packages to her husband that was in a-- in a labor in the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: She wanted you to go to the Soviet Union for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the one-- my advisor, Mr. Gelman, [POLISH] [? Gelman, ?] which I later stayed with them, said no way are you going to do that. And I moved in with them. I slept on a-- on a clothes box. You know, [POLISH] if you know. That is a Polish word.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you what it was like to return to Hoszcza.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was really-- it was really not believable. But I was so used to-- to the bad things, that it was a little bit hard to-- to be free. It was-- it was unreal. Always looking over your shoulder.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you greeted by the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hoszcza? There were very few, but they were very happy. They survived in-- in the villages. They survived in-- in barns. And they had their farmer's dug for them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunkers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunkers or graves, I would say. And people-- like, five people in one-- in one bunker [INAUDIBLE]. A cow was on top of them. In other words, after it was dug, the cow had-- it was covered, covered well, and they were-- the-- the family that survived, I stayed with them in Hoszcza. I also--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the Poles and the Ukrainians saw you? When they saw that some Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too safe to be in the town of Hoszcza. As a matter of fact, when-- when the teacher [INAUDIBLE] saw me, she said that you would be better off not to go to see the graves of your-- the grave of your father or other graves. They were right not too far from her home. That was the Jewish cemetery. My mother had no grave, and I also found out from those shoemakers that my sister had perished. My sister Leah had perished. And I-- and I-- I really wasn't afraid. I was not afraid because I felt that it couldn't be worse than what I went through. When my husband came to-- my-- my husband now, 50 years, he came to the town of Hoszcza, he was told to leave Hoszcza as fast as he can because it was unsafe, because the Ukranians were-- were not to be trusted. So I worked in a-- in a savings bank that they-- they established, the Soviet government. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in Hoszcza?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Hoszcza. [INAUDIBLE] you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said with a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a family, with a Jewish Hoszcza family that survived in the conditions that I told you. And they-- I stayed with them at first in a-- I worked in a restaurant that he established for the workers, Mr. [? Gelman ?] established.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was-- it was safe enough for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was safe enough because the Soviet government knew exactly who they are dealing with. And they-- when they went to their villages, they were well-armed. On top of it, they knew that the [INAUDIBLE] are not quiet yet, and the Soviet Union would definitely not give them permission for a-- for an independent Ukraine. There was no such thing. There was an Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Jews? The few Jews would not venture into the villages. I wouldn't say that. The only ones that went was the armed-- you know, the militia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Military.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not safe for us now not from the Germans, but from the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So how long did-- you said you worked in a restaurant, and then in a bank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very shortly they established a savings bank the Soviet government. And the savings bank was on the outskirts of Hoszcza, so my girlfriend, [POLISH], and I worked in that savings bank. She was the cashier and I was the comptroller. And I-- and I hitched rides with the cash to the town of Rowne to deliver the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Hoszcza working?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hoszcza, we stayed in Hoszcza until the Soviet Union decided on a repatriation. So all Jews-- all Jews and all Poles have to leave the eastern part of Poland. Ukraine and Polesia and [POLISH]-- all those parts had to be cleared of Jews and repatriated to the new Poland. Now, their Poland was also influenced by them. It was really under their auspices, but it was called the new Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So by trains we were taken. I left with the family together, with the-- with the family [? Gelman ?]. And-- and the way in Rowne, the train stopped and then other Jews joined us. Where the Poles traveled, I was not with them. And I have never met again the Polish family, but they, too, repatriated. They repatriated to close to the town where we landed. We landed in Bytom. Many Jews settled in the Lodz, and Lodz had a committee. And the committee was-- had a lot of, you know, registered Jews-- whoever was from the outskirts of Poland and the survivors. So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Bytom?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bytom, we heard that [POLISH], my friend from home, has arrived to Lodz, an in Lodz he heard that we are in Bytom. So he and his brother Harvey-- at that time he was [POLISH]-- and his-- and his-- and his wife. She was Russian-born Jewish lady. They came to Bytom to look for us and they settled in Bytom also. And of course it was good to see my boyfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he your boyfriend before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He taught me how to ski and-- and-- and-- and ice skate and bicycle-ride. And we went to school together, but since he was two years older, so he was in-- in higher grades than I was, also in the [INAUDIBLE] school. And as far as getting married, I had-- you know, I was torn between Palestine, where my brother was, and between my sister that still remained in Russia. And she sent me papers and he sent me papers. She-- it was a [POLISH], which is like, you know, you're asked to join the family in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wouldn't go back to Russia. I knew very well. And I did not care for the communists. After, especially, they nationalized our beautiful home and business, why would I want to go back to Russia? So it was a toss-up between going to Palestine. But there-- it wasn't illegal immigration to Palestine. And there was an organization called Brigade, so we very easily could have, you know, joined them. And-- What kind of an organization was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brigade was the Jews could travel from country to country until they would come to Italy or somewhere close where, you know, an illegal immigration was being organized, and they would join them.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Palestine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I had a proposal from my-- from my boyfriend to go to Palestine, and I said, no, I'm not going. I am not-- as long as we are not married, I'm not going. And just it was like, I am proposing to him. And he said fine, let's get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my trusted adviser, [INAUDIBLE] [? Gelman-- ?] I asked, what should I do? I had-- had these suitors, you know, that were much older. And he said, I cannot advise you about the others, but for [POLISH], by all means. And we got married in Bytom in their home. It was an apartment. Tell me about your emotions the day you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were no family, just strangers. And the [? Gelman ?] family was there. My husband's brother, his wife. My very good friend [? Rachelle ?] [? Gelman ?]. She was [POLISH] from-- her maiden name was. She was with her boyfriend. That's how she survived. The boyfriend-- [POLISH] [? Gelman ?] said I will not go to hide unless my girlfriend [? Rachelle ?] goes with me, and that's how they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they had a very nice dinner prepared. And my only cousin, the pharmacist-- she had a pharmacy in nearby town. Or I think it was [POLISH] or [POLISH], and she came with her husband. That was the only family we had. Practical strangers that my husband met on the way from Russia during his stay in Russia. And whoever was in Bytom joined the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bytom we left for Czechoslovakia. We thought that we had right passports, that we had legitimate passports, but we didn't, and we were arrested in Czechoslovakia. It was a Soviet government already, and they searched us. And they searched my husband, then me. And we-- they didn't find anything. They worried that we are young spies. But then they saw that if they didn't find anything, apparently we are not, and they freed us. And we joined--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a brief thing. It was a brief thing. We went by train from Poland to Czechoslovakia, thinking that we are safe to cross borders. Now, the Polish government didn't really mind, and crossing of their border, they made no difficulty. They were happy to have a few less Jews there. And we left also for the reason that the pogram was at that time in [POLISH]. And no way did we want to stay any longer, waiting for papers from-- from-- from America, which my husband had a lot of family and I had an uncle here. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakian government did not-- did not think that the passports are legitimate, and this is why we were jailed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had you gotten them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got them to-- my cousin. You know, whether he knew that they are legitimate or not we don't know. We paid-- whatever money we had we paid for them, and he made them for us, the cousin the pharmacist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you heading?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heading towards-- thinking that maybe-- we really didn't know where we were heading. There was no plans. No plans. But we knew that if we come to Germany, there are displaced person camps there. We figured that maybe from there would either we go to America or we go to Palestine when the time is right and when the immigration is open. And so we met the head of the Brigade. My husband knew him from Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew him from Russia. And he said to follow him to Austria. There were-- there were borders, and we did that at night. And we came--

speaker: interviewer
text: He took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and he-- not just us, many other Jewish people-- young, mainly. And we came to the town of Linz that was on the Danube. That's where we had our honeymoon because he gave us a special room, you know. And we stayed there, and later traveled to Germany. And we settled in Ulm. Ulm on the Danube was a camp, a displaced person camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about life there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '46 already. 1946. We married in February 3rd of 1946, and at that same time, we traveled and arrived-- still '46, we arrived into the town of Ulm. There were two camps in Ulm. One camp was in town. One displaced camp was in town where the [? Gelman ?] family lived, and one camp was in out of town in Ulm. They were camps-- GERMAN, you know, where military used to be stationed. And my husband was, there, a block leader. The population was 1,000 Jewish people, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did a block leader do?

speaker: interviewee
text: A block leader was, you know, making sure that the food was supplied. It was all rationed. But the [GERMAN] supplied food. And there was an [INAUDIBLE] organized, and he-- and he was studying in, also, some English, and I studied English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- at that time I was-- we had one in the room with another family, and I was pregnant with our older daughter Linda. And that's where I gave birth to our first daughter. There was no question already of traveling illegally to Palestine, today's Israel. And we waited patiently for the papers from America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends in the DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of friends, yes. A lot of friends. Some of them left for Australia. Yes. And some of them left for Israel, although illegally. And they were stationed in Cyprus later and came-- finally came to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there two years, from '46 until-- '46 until-- more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you said earlier til August '49.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, until August '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: So three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years. In '47--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a life was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --'47 Linda was born, our older daughter was born. And life-- it was, you know, like always waiting for the ration. You know, not much to do. But yet, you know, between the Jewish people, there was even performances in the evenings. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you resume any religious observances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right then and there in Bytom. In Bytom, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a synagogue in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In DP camp there was a place of worship. I wouldn't-- you know, place of worship. And back again in Hoszcza when I was freed, the remaining large synagogue, the [POLISH], it was still there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the torahs still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so, no. We had three synagogues. One was the very big one that my family belonged, one was for the-- small one for the working people. And another one was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to the United States in 1949 in August with your husband and daughter. Tell me what happened when you arrived, when you landed here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We met-- we were met by cousins. Now, it was not that easy to get to America because every time we would show up to the American consulate, he would want to know where the town Hoszcza is on the map, and we couldn't find it, so we told him it's near Rowne, 30 kilometers. What government was it under? Was it Polish, Russian? And that was all because of a quarter. If it was the Russian quarter, it was all-- it was all used up. Polish quarter. So I patiently explained that it was 1920, it was Poland until '39, it was until '20, and before that, it was the Russian government-- the czarist Russia. And he said-- well, he wasn't sure which quarter I belonged to, so it was difficult to get to America. And we kept on getting papers from the cousins very patiently all the time, changing-- changing names, changing family. There was an addition. There was a daughter, Linda.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he-- but we got the papers, the new ones. And finally, no quarter helped, but the International Refugee Organization was established and took us-- you know, even the boats, the passage was free. Passage was free. And we arrived [INAUDIBLE] to America. We were met with, you know, just cousins that we never met, but I used to write to them in English. From the peace camp I wrote to them to-- to-- to America, and I also wrote to my uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he must have been agnostic because every time we said, 'Thank God, thank God,' he would tell my family, I don't know why she always talks about God. He was a bachelor. Was a very, very nice human being. And he prepared an apartment for us and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we couldn't come, so he had to give it up. There was great difficulty getting apartments here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you got here--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he thought he'll prepare it. He'll prepare it, yes. When we got, we lived in a one-room apartment. And my uncle came, and he saw-- no, that wouldn't do. So we moved to a furnished basement in Brighton Beach, and we stayed there. And Linda spoke Yiddish-- Linda, our daughter, spoke Yiddish. And she played with a friend and she learned English, but she spoke Yiddish for a whole year. And then she learned English also. We stayed in this-- this basement apartment until we got an apartment in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you move? When did you have your second child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Second child was born in 1952 in the Bronx, and she was born in Mt. Eden hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Sheila. [NON-ENGLISH] [SOBBING] She is named after my husband's father, [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH] is her great grandfather-- the one that was in [POLISH], my mother's father. And since I wrote to my brother, he said [NON-ENGLISH] is the right name. That was her Hebrew name. And she-- of course, in America, it was Sheila Beatrice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you resume your education at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not right away, no, but I studied English always at home in private and improved it. And my husband Irving worked for the Hudson paper company for many years, for 30 years. They [INAUDIBLE] one of ours, and he got the job there. We lived in the Bronx until '59.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you move here to Fairlawn

speaker: interviewee
text: To Fairlawn, yes. Our children went to PS7 in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll stop here and continue in a minute.
